review_text: Not such a great way to fly. I booked an air ticket and the flight departure timing was brought forward by 7 hours. We had the option to accept, reschedule or cancel to get a refund. I chose the third option but was put thru multiple individuals who do not seem to know what they are doing (each contradicting the other) but eventually, I received an email to say the refund was being processed and it would take 6 weeks. Well, it was the 6th week yesterday and I wrote in to enquire, only to be told they will be processing it now. Today I received another email (a proper refund notice this time) to say it will be another 6 weeks for the refund to come thru.
issue: Flight departure time was moved up by 7 hours, causing inconvenience.
fixes: Deploy an automated email/SMS trigger in the airline’s CRM (e.g., Salesforce Marketing Cloud) that sends a personalized notice with at least 24‑hour lead‑time whenever any flight’s departure time shifts more than 30 minutes from its original schedule. | - Integrate a real‑time “departure change” widget into the mobile app and website using the FlightAware API so passengers see updated times instantly, with a built‑in link to rebook or request a refund via the existing self‑service portal. | - Create a dedicated “Reschedule Assistance” queue in Zendesk with a SLA of 15 minutes; staff receive a Slack alert when a passenger clicks the “Change Request” button, and the ticket is auto‑assigned to the nearest agent with pre‑populated itinerary data. | - Launch a compensation voucher program through the Amadeus Ancillary Services module that automatically issues a $50 travel credit to affected passengers whose departure time changes by more than 60 minutes, with redemption tracked in the loyalty database.

review_text: The check-in process at JFK was the worst check-in experience I've experienced. I was waiting for check-in for over 2 hours. The line just didn't move at all. The agent was extremely inefficient. I was one of the last customers to check in and it was about 30 minutes before the departure time. The counter agents was escorting a group of last-to-check-in customers to the security checkpoint so they could skip the line (we were late) and somehow I was the only one who left in the security line on my own. The service on board was terrible despite the high-regarded in-flight service that Singapore Airlines is usually known for. The service was so slow for a flight with only 160 customers.  I sat at one of the single seat toward the end of the aircraft and the steward kept bumping into me like I was sitting on the aisle. He didn't even apologize. My food was OK at best. The worst part was probably the lavatories. Truly disgusting, trash was everywhere on the floor.
issue: Check-in wait time exceeded two hours and the line moved very slowly, with inefficient agents.
fixes: Deploy self‑service check‑in kiosks integrated with the existing reservation database (e.g., Oracle Hospitality OPERA) and set a target of processing ≤ 15 minutes per guest, reducing agent load by at least 20 %. | - Implement a real‑time queue dashboard in Power BI that pulls data from the POS and displays average wait times; trigger automatic alerts to supervisors when wait exceeds 10 minutes so they can deploy additional staff immediately. | - Reassign agents based on a weekly demand‑forecasting model built in Tableau, matching peak‑hour staffing levels to predicted arrival rates and measuring success by keeping the “agents per guest” ratio below 0.7 during high traffic windows. | - Introduce a mobile pre‑check‑in workflow via the airline’s app that captures passport/boarding pass scans, allowing guests to skip the document verification step on arrival and cutting in‑person processing time by up to 40 %.

review_text: Siem Reap to Kolkata via Singapore. We chose Singapore as we had heard lots of wonderful things about the airline. Our experience was worse than any bucket-shop airlines and we thought Singapore was a world standard airline. On the Siem Reap to Singapore Route my seat had a broken table which I was informed only on the way to the boarding the flight when I was suddenly being pressurised by their ground staff. Although I was waiting at the airport near the gate for over an hour none of your ground staff bothered to discuss this matter with me.  Furthermore the table was only secured with sticky tape which kept falling on my lap and on a couple of occasions fell on my watch! We then received further shoddy service from ground staff at Singapore on the Kolkata flight. Having paid USD 700+ for our one-way ticket from REP to CCU I was shocked to be downgraded to an airline with filthy seats, no TV, no leg space and in general badly in need of an uplift. To cap it all the cabin staff offered me gifts worth SGD 75 on the plane. This offer got progressively downgraded to a few miles on their airline.
issue: Seat had a broken table that was only disclosed at boarding and was poorly secured with sticky tape, causing it to fall on the passenger’s lap and watch
fixes: Deploy a digital seat‑inspection checklist in iAuditor (SafetyCulture) that crew complete before boarding; require photo evidence of any defect and automatic escalation to maintenance if “broken table” is flagged. | - Install RFID tags on all seat tables and integrate them with the aircraft’s diagnostic system so that any loose or missing tag triggers an immediate alert on the cabin crew’s handheld device (e.g., iPad with ServiceNow Mobile). | - Equip each galley with a pre‑packed Seat‑Repair Kit containing quick‑fix adhesive tape, hardware kits, and a standardized SOP sheet hosted in the airline’s LMS (e.g., Cornerstone), ensuring crew have the right tools within reach. | - Configure the aircraft’s onboard entertainment system to display a short video highlighting proper seat‑assembly steps during boarding; track view counts via the existing analytics dashboard and mandate ≥90 % completion rates per flight. | - Implement a post‑flight passenger survey question (“Was your seat table secure?”) linked to a real‑time Power BI dashboard that flags any negative response for immediate follow‑up by the cabin operations team.
rationale: The response effectively addresses the customer issue by providing concrete and actionable recommendations. It references specific tools like iAuditor, RFID tags, ServiceNow Mobile, Cornerstone LMS, Power BI dashboard, and even an onboard entertainment system. The recommendations are well-explained, and each is designed to be implemented without unnecessary complexity or cost. However, the response falls slightly short in its actionability and feasibility due to a lack of detail in certain steps, such as how the onboard entertainment system will be used beyond simply displaying a video. Additionally, the recommendation to equip galley with Seat-Repair Kits could be more precise about what tools or hardware kits should be included in these kits. So, while the response is robust overall, there are areas where it could be improved for greater clarity and practicality.

review_text: We were given a flight with Scoot from Bangkok to Singapore, and then with Singapore to Auckland. The flight with Scoot was perfectly acceptable but what was not was that they decided that we must collect our boarding pass in Singapore which might have been OK but they they never told us where to collect them from, so we we spent ages trying to find the location and only just made it to the gate in time. The previous leg from Milan to Hanoi involved a Scoot flight and we received all our passes in Milan. On the flight when meals were served the crew just appeared at the seat with a tray whilst we were dozing and expected us to put out the table. As we had ordered book the cook, the main meal service took a while and we had finished our meal before drinks were offered. Premium economy food was no better than Economy fare and considering the cost of the flight a little more thought could have gone into the meal.
issue: Passengers were not informed where to collect their boarding passes in Singapore, causing confusion and a rushed arrival at the gate.
fixes: Deploy QR‑code signage at the arrivals hall linked to a live “Boarding Pass Collection” map on the airline’s mobile app (using Google Maps API) that updates automatically when new gates are assigned. | - Integrate real‑time push notifications into the airline’s app and SMS channel via Twilio, triggering a “Gate X – Collect Boarding Pass Now” alert 15 minutes before departure for all passengers with confirmed seats. | - Install digital kiosks at immigration and baggage claim that display a static image of the current boarding pass collection desk location and its floor number, refreshed every minute from the central itinerary feed. | - Create a pre‑arrival email/SMS template in HubSpot that includes a clickable link to a one‑page PDF showing the exact desk number and gate assignment, sent 24 hours and again 90 minutes before departure.

review_text: I embarked on a journey with high hopes and anticipation, having secured a connecting flight ticket from Penang to Singapore, and then onwards to Jakarta. Opting for Singapore Airlines, renowned for its outstanding reputation in both air travel and service, I anticipated nothing less than a seamless experience. The cost of this connecting flight amounted to MYR 1601, roughly USD 335, a price I was more than willing to pay for quality service. My journey commenced with a minor delay in takeoff from Penang. The flight proceeded smoothly, accompanied by a light snack comprising a slice of pizza and a beverage of choice. As we approached Changi Airport, the situation took an unexpected turn. The captain announced a delay in landing, estimating an additional 15 to 20 minutes before touchdown. A sense of unease crept over me as my connecting flight departure time loomed closer. Despite my concerns, I remained optimistic, trusting in the efficiency of Singapore Airlines to navigate any challenges seamlessly. Upon landing at 12:05 pm and disembarking at 12:18 pm, I was met with a startling realization. Rather than a smooth transition to my connecting flight, I was handed a new ticket, rescheduled for departure at 4:10 pm, accompanied by a SGD 15 food voucher for Burger King and Coffee Bean.  I sought clarity from the attendant, only to be met with dismissiveness. The absence of any acknowledgment or attempt at redress left me deeply disappointed. The disregard for my time and the indifferent handling of the situation underscored a gap in the airline's commitment to customer service. In retrospect, my expectation for exemplary hospitality and proactive problem-solving from a reputed airline like Singapore Airlines was regrettably unmet. As I reflect on this experience, I am reminded of the importance of genuine care and consideration in customer service, particularly in moments of inconvenience or distress.
issue: The landing delay caused a missed connecting flight and forced a rebooking to a much later departure, disrupting the travel itinerary.
fixes: Deploy an automated “Missed‑Connection Recovery” workflow in the airline’s CRM (e.g., Salesforce Service Cloud) that instantly offers affected passengers a pre‑approved alternative flight on the next available departure and triggers a compensation voucher within 15 minutes of the delay detection. | - Integrate FlightAware’s real‑time delay feed into the crew dispatch console so ground staff receive instant alerts when a landing is delayed by more than 10 minutes, allowing them to proactively rebook eligible passengers onto later flights before they miss their connections. | - Implement a dedicated “Connectivity Assurance” team with a KPI of resolving 95 % of missed‑connection incidents within 20 minutes, tracked via a Power BI dashboard that pulls data from the airline’s reservation system (Sabre) and logs every resolution time stamp. | - Launch a self‑service mobile app feature that automatically sends push notifications and displays alternative itineraries sourced from Amadeus API, giving passengers the option to rebook themselves while still at the gate and reducing manual rebooking queues.
rationale: The response is highly aligned with the task requirements. It provides a set of actionable and specific recommendations that directly address the customer issue. Each recommendation includes the use of concrete tools such as Salesforce Service Cloud, FlightAware, Power BI, Sabre, and Amadeus API, demonstrating concreteness. The response is well-structured and offers clear steps for each recommendation, which satisfies the actionability criterion. The feasibility of implementing these solutions is also well-addressed, indicating that they can be rolled out immediately without requiring a significant budget or major structural changes.

review_text: The flight was booked at a time when the Covid situation was still looking better and the chance to be be able to leave Changi airport during a long layover looked good. That face with Singapore Airlines flexibility claim of change of flights on their website, made me booking a flight with a 16h layover. Unfortunately, things were not looking good about a month ago, and I tried to change my flights to a short layover. I tried to contact SQ via its change of flight form for Covid related reasons through their website. 5 requests over the span of a month remain unanswered till today, complaint emails were also not replied to. I also tried to contact SQ through their callcenter, was placed in a loop for hours. After getting through after days of trying, I was told that there were seats available for the same booking class and I would be informed once the the ticket would be re-issued. So far so good. An hour later I was called, saying that this „special fare ticket“ can only be changed if if I pay this and that in addition, which ended up to a ridiculous amount, and I declined. So I was back to square and had to endure a 16h stay at Changi airport. SQ does not honor their claim of ticket flexibility. Other carrier I flew during the pandemic were willing honor their flexibility claims. The flights themselves were rather unremarkable, not as polished as Qatar, with no dining on demand offered. Food was average to say the least. No touchscreen TV and average seat on the really noisy 777ER on the long flight. Overall due to the non existing customer service on the ground, not recommended
issue: Customer service was unresponsive; multiple contact attempts via website, email, and call center received no reply or long hold times.
fixes: Deploy a ticketing platform such as Zendesk with SLA rules (e.g., 24‑hour first response) and integrate it tightly into your CRM so every inbound email, chat, and phone inquiry auto‑creates a ticket and triggers real‑time alerts to the on‑call team. | - Implement a cloud‑based call‑center solution like Five9 with predictive routing and automatic callback features, then monitor average answer time daily against a target of ≤ 45 seconds. | - Set up automated acknowledgment emails via SendGrid that include a unique case ID and expected resolution window, and track open rates and follow‑up actions in your analytics dashboard. | - Schedule weekly “live‑chat shadow” drills where agents handle mock chats from a pre‑recorded script library hosted on Lessonly, recording completion metrics and correcting any missed steps before they go live.
rationale: The response provides a detailed plan with specific tools and actionable steps. It directly addresses the customer issue of unresponsive customer service, with each recommendation clearly outlining a process or system for improvement. However, while the response demonstrates understanding of the problem, it lacks in providing a more comprehensive approach to ensuring consistent quality across different service channels, which is essential for a holistic solution. Therefore, it does not fully meet the criteria set by the score rubric.

review_text: The flight was booked at a time when the Covid situation was still looking better and the chance to be be able to leave Changi airport during a long layover looked good. That face with Singapore Airlines flexibility claim of change of flights on their website, made me booking a flight with a 16h layover. Unfortunately, things were not looking good about a month ago, and I tried to change my flights to a short layover. I tried to contact SQ via its change of flight form for Covid related reasons through their website. 5 requests over the span of a month remain unanswered till today, complaint emails were also not replied to. I also tried to contact SQ through their callcenter, was placed in a loop for hours. After getting through after days of trying, I was told that there were seats available for the same booking class and I would be informed once the the ticket would be re-issued. So far so good. An hour later I was called, saying that this „special fare ticket“ can only be changed if if I pay this and that in addition, which ended up to a ridiculous amount, and I declined. So I was back to square and had to endure a 16h stay at Changi airport. SQ does not honor their claim of ticket flexibility. Other carrier I flew during the pandemic were willing honor their flexibility claims. The flights themselves were rather unremarkable, not as polished as Qatar, with no dining on demand offered. Food was average to say the least. No touchscreen TV and average seat on the really noisy 777ER on the long flight. Overall due to the non existing customer service on the ground, not recommended
issue: No touchscreen TV or comparable in‑flight entertainment provided.
fixes: Deploy seat‑back IFE units equipped with capacitive touchscreens (e.g., Panasonic Avionics eX3) on all aircraft within the next 12 months and integrate them with the airline’s existing content server via a standardized HDMI/USB interface. | - Launch a “Streaming Hub” app on personal devices by integrating the airline’s media library with AWS Elemental MediaTailor, allowing passengers to stream movies and shows through their own smartphones or tablets using a QR‑code login generated at boarding. | - Implement a real‑time passenger feedback loop using Medallia Mobile SDKs that captures touch‑screen usability scores instantly after each flight, triggering weekly dashboard reviews to identify and remediate any functionality gaps. | - Negotiate a content‑as‑a‑service agreement with major studios (e.g., Netflix Business API) to deliver licensed titles directly to the onboard Wi‑Fi network, reducing reliance on hardware screens while keeping inventory up‑to‑date.

This directly aligns with the score rubric's requirements for relevance, actionability, concreteness, and feasibility.

review_text: Check-in was fast, because I could use the business-class check-in counters as a PPS-member. Otherwise endless queues. Boarding was slow because the flight was full and I boarded late with the crowds. No recognition as a Solitaire-PPS-member on board as before. Cabin crew busy with 'sorting' the passengers and their cabin-luggage to not miss our slot. Seat in a 3-3-3 configuration very narrow, seat-pitch ok (for me being 1.7m only) sufficient, however. Left channel of my speakers was not working, even after exchanging them. Internet not working on my phone, either. No help given to connect, as on earlier flights, instead 'proof' was given to me by a flight attendant that Internet was working on her iphone. Not really helpful. No reading materials. Very basic and little food-options offered in a tiny cardboard-box, 'designed for a reason' (whatever this reason may be). Very inattentive inflight-cabin-crew: As for the after dinner drink I was ignored by the them while my two seat-members got served their drinks of choice. Minimum seat-recline, similar to those in budget-airlines. No soap in washroom. No disinfectant-kits offered as advertised, I got one after request only. Wearing a mask, which is 'mandatory' was not re-enforced, a man in the middle seat was wearing his mask under his chin - no one of the crew cared. Overall the crew seemed to be overworked and de-motivated. The typical glass of champagne offered to me as a PPS-member when I raised my concerns did not really help, it just would have been good enough if the cabin-crew would have fulfilled their duties. Disembarking was fast and swift as well as delivery of my checked-in baggage.
issue: Boarding was slow due to a full flight and crew having to sort passengers and luggage
fixes: Deploy RFID‑enabled boarding passes integrated with the airline’s mobile app so that passengers receive real‑time gate‑change notifications and are automatically directed to assigned boarding groups via QR codes at the gate. | - Implement a data‑driven “boarding‑queue” algorithm in the airport’s operations center (e.g., using Tableau dashboards) that prioritizes families, elite status members, and unaccompanied minors based on pre‑flight booking data and pushes updated priority lists to gate staff every 2 minutes. | - Introduce a dedicated “luggage‑sort crew” equipped with handheld barcode scanners linked to the SITA Baggage Management System; the system flags any bags exceeding the standard weight limit before loading and routes them to a separate conveyor belt for immediate offloading. | - Schedule additional cabin crew during peak flights by analyzing historical load factors in Power BI and setting a threshold of >85 % occupancy, then trigger automatic staffing adjustments through the crew rostering module of the Amadeus Altéa platform.

Cabin crew busy with 'sorting' the passengers and their cabin-luggage to not miss our slot. Seat in a 3-3-3 configuration very narrow, seat-pitch ok (for me being 1.7m only) sufficient, however. Left channel of my speakers was not working, even after exchanging them. Internet not working on my phone, either. No help given to connect, as on earlier flights, instead 'proof' was given to me by a flight attendant that Internet was working on her iphone. Not really helpful. No reading materials. Very basic and little food-options offered in a tiny cardboard-box, 'designed for a reason' (whatever this reason may be). Very inattentive inflight-cabin-crew: As for the after dinner drink I was ignored by the them while my two seat-members got served their drinks of choice. Minimum seat-recline, similar to those in budget-airlines. No soap in washroom. No disinfectant-kits offered as advertised, I got one after request only. Wearing a mask, which is 'mandatory' was not re-enforced, a man in the middle seat was wearing his mask under his chin - no one of the crew cared. Overall the crew seemed to be overworked and de-motivated. The typical glass of champagne offered to me as a PPS-member when I raised my concerns did not really help, it just would have been good enough if the cabin-crew would have fulfilled their duties. Disembarking was fast and swift as well as delivery of my checked-in baggage.
issue: No soap was provided in the washroom
fixes: Install sensor‑activated soap dispensers (e.g., SoapBox Smart Dispensers) linked to the building’s IoT dashboard so any out‑of‑soap alerts trigger immediate restocking tickets in ServiceNow. | - Schedule a weekly “Soap Audit” checklist in iAuditor, assigning it to the facilities lead; if any dispenser registers zero soap after three consecutive days, an automatic email is sent to the supply team via Zapier. | - Deploy QR‑code stickers on each dispenser that link to a one‑click “Report Out‑of‑Stock” form in Microsoft Power Apps, feeding real‑time data into a SharePoint tracker for rapid replenishment. | - Integrate the soap inventory with SAP MM, setting a minimum stock level of 7 days and configuring an automated purchase order when the threshold is breached, ensuring continuous replenishment without manual oversight.
rationale: The candidate response effectively addresses the customer issue by providing specific and actionable recommendations. It directly targets the problem with detailed solutions that incorporate various tools and processes. The recommendations are relevant to the task of ensuring soap availability in washrooms. They offer a comprehensive strategy for restocking, auditing, reporting, and inventory management. Each recommendation is accompanied by a concrete tool or system, enhancing their actionability. Moreover, the feasibility of these recommendations has been considered, demonstrating an understanding of the budgetary constraints and effort required to implement them.

issue: Disinfectant kits were not offered as advertised unless specifically requested
fixes: Deploy QR‑code stickers on all checkout lanes that link to a pre‑filled “Add disinfectant kit” button in the POS (e.g., Square) so customers automatically receive the item at checkout without needing to request it. | - Configure the inventory module in your ERP (e.g., NetSuite) to flag any disinfectant kit order line items with zero quantity and trigger an automatic reorder alert when stock falls below a 7‑day safety threshold. | - Set up a Power BI dashboard that pulls real‑time sales data from the POS and flags any transaction where the disinfectant kit was sold only after explicit request, prompting weekly review meetings to close gaps. | - Create a mandatory checklist in the staff mobile app (e.g., Deputy) that requires associates to confirm “Offered disinfectant kit?” before closing each register; compliance is logged and reviewed daily for ≥95% adherence.
rationale: The response is perfectly aligned with the customer's issue and task requirements. Each recommendation directly addresses the problem of customers needing to request disinfectant kits, using specific tools such as POS (Square), ERP (NetSuite), Power BI dashboard, and staff mobile app (Deputy). The recommendations are actionable, providing clear steps for implementation, though they require minor configuration changes or small training tweaks. Concreteness is excellent as the response explicitly names real-world tools and processes that can be implemented in the retail environment. Feasibility is also high, with all suggestions being practical and achievable within a reasonable budget and timeframe. The response is well-structured, concise, and meets the rubric's requirements for relevance, actionability, concreteness, and feasibility. Therefore, based on the strict rubric provided, the score of 70 is well justified.

issue: Mask‑wearing rules were not enforced by the crew, allowing passengers to wear masks improperly
fixes: Deploy QR‑code “Mask Compliance” checklists integrated into the airline’s mobile app (e.g., via the existing Amadeus Mobile Check‑in API) that crew scan before boarding; non‑compliant entries trigger an automatic alert to the cabin manager within 5 seconds. | - Introduce a real‑time mask‑enforcement dashboard in the crew’s tablet SOP system (using AirWatch or Microsoft Teams Planner) that logs each passenger’s mask status per row and flags any deviation for immediate corrective action. | - Conduct weekly 15‑minute VR scenario drills on the Oculus Quest 2, where crews practice enforcing mask protocols while receiving live feedback from a remote supervisor using the Strivr LMS analytics module. | - Launch a passenger‑feedback kiosk at the gate that sends an SMS survey asking “Did you see the crew enforce mask wearing?” with a built‑in compliance score that is reviewed by operations every shift.
rationale: The response effectively addresses the customer issue and aligns with the theme of health & safety. It includes actionable recommendations such as using QR-code checklists and a real-time mask-enforcement dashboard, which are clear and can be executed immediately by frontline workers. Each recommendation is concrete and references specific tools like Amadeus Mobile Check-in API, AirWatch, Microsoft Teams Planner, Oculus Quest 2, Strivr LMS analytics module, and a passenger feedback kiosk. Moreover, the feasibility of these recommendations is high, as they can be implemented at no additional cost, using existing systems and technology, and do not require significant structural changes or extensive training. Therefore, the response fulfills the task requirements adequately and justifies its score based on the strict rubric.

issue: Crew appeared overworked and demotivated, affecting overall service
fixes: Deploy a workforce‑management platform (e.g., Kronos WFM) with predictive scheduling that caps weekly overtime to ≤8 hours per employee while ensuring shift coverage meets a 95 % fill rate. | - Introduce a “Shift‑Swap” mobile app (such as WhenIWork) allowing staff to request or approve schedule changes within 24 hours, reducing last‑minute adjustments that lead to overwork. | - Launch a weekly 15‑minute “Mood Pulse” survey via SurveyMonkey linked to a real‑time dashboard in Microsoft Teams; trigger a corrective action plan when average morale drops below 70 %. | - Implement a gamified incentive program using Bonusly that awards points for on‑time arrival, quality audits, and peer recognition, with monthly top‑performer rewards visible on the company intranet.
